MohammadParsaElahimanesh's blog

Codeforces Global Round 31
By MohammadParsaElahimanesh, 3 months ago,

Salam !

Here is the editorial for Codeforces Global Round 31. We hope you find these solutions helpful. We also have prepared a special editorial with step by step hint, examples, tables, and walkthroughs which is available here.

2180A - Carnival Wheel

Idea: AmirrzwM, MohammadParsaElahimanesh — Preparation: MohammadParsaElahimanesh

Solution
Simple Simulation (Brute force)

Since constraints are small, we can simulate the spins directly:

  • Start from section aa.
  • Move the pointer by adding bb modulo ll repeatedly.
  • Stop when we return to a previously visited section.
  • Among all visited sections, select the section with the highest prize.

It runs in O(l)O(l) as we mark each section at most once.

Mathematical Solution (Using GCD)

It's easy to see that the pointer can land on section cc if c≡a(modd)c≡a(modd), where d=gcd(l,b)d=gcd(l,b). By Bézout's lemma, all such cc are indeed reachable. Hence, the maximum attainable section is:

l−d+(amodd).l−d+(amodd).
Implementation
// In the name of God
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
int main() {
    int l, a, b, t;
    cin >> t;
    while(t--) {
        cin >> l >> a >> b;
        cout << l - __gcd(l, b) + a % __gcd(l, b) << '\n'; 
    }
    return 0;
}

2180B - Ashmal

Idea: AmirrzwM, MohammadParsaElahimanesh — Preparation: MohammadParsaElahimanesh

Solution

  • Step 1: It can be shown (for instance, via a contradiction argument) that, to obtain the lexicographically minimum final string ss, we must ensure ss is lexicographically minimum at each intermediate step as well.

  • Step 2: Therefore, at every step ii, we choose the lexicographically smaller result between adding aiai to the beginning or the end of our current string.

  • Step 3: Initially, ss is the empty string. Suppose before step ii, the current string is sisi. Then the best choice for the updated string is:

min(si+ai, ai+si).min(si+ai, ai+si).
  • Step 4: By applying this choice at each step, we can compute the final (minimum) string in O(nS)O(nS) where SS is the sum of string lengths.
Implementation
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
 
using namespace std;
 
int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false), cin.tie(nullptr);
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while(t--) {
        int n;
        cin >> n;
        string s[n], t;
        for(int i = 0; i < n; i++)
            cin >> s[i];
        for(int i = 0; i < n; i++)
            t = min(t+s[i], s[i]+t);
        cout << t << '\n';
    }
    return 0;
}

2180C - XOR-factorization

Idea: MohammadParsaElahimanesh — Preparation: MohammadParsaElahimanesh

Solution

We build a1,…,aka1,…,ak bit by bit from the highest bit to 00.

Let

o(x)=max{t≤x: t odd},e(x)=max{t≤x: t even}.o(x)=max{t≤x: t odd},e(x)=max{t≤x: t even}.

Maintain two sets of indices:

  • TT (tight): current prefix of aiai equals the prefix of nn,

  • LL (loose): current prefix of aiai is already smaller than the prefix of nn.

Initially, T=1,…,kT=1,…,k, L=∅L=∅, and all ai=0ai=0.

Greedy rule for each bit bb (from high to low).

  • If the bb-th bit of nn is 11, then the number of aiai having bit bb equal to 11 must be odd. To maximize the sum we set as many ones as possible, i.e. exactly o(k)o(k). So:

    • if kk is odd, set this bit in all kk numbers;
    • if kk is even, set it in all but one number. Prefer choosing that one from TT (if possible), because then it becomes loose (T→LT→L), which can help later.

  • If the bb-th bit of nn is 00, then the number of ones in this bit must be even. Tight indices cannot put 11 here (otherwise ai>nai>n), so only LL may receive ones. Thus we set this bit in exactly e(|L|)e(|L|) loose numbers (all of them if |L||L| is even, otherwise all but one).

Why it is optimal. At bit bb, each extra chosen 11 adds 2b2b to the total sum, and this gain is larger than anything we can get from lower bits. Also, the XOR condition fixes the parity in every bit, so we can only change the number of 11’s by removing them from an even number of elements. So, at every bit we should keep as many 11’s as possible, as long as we do not violate ai≤nai≤n. When the bb-th bit of nn is 11 and kk is even, we must place one 00; choosing it from a tight index increases |L||L|, which can help us place more 11’s later when nn has 00-bits.

This directly gives a construction with 0≤ai≤n0≤ai≤n, XOR equal to nn, and maximum possible sum in O(klogn)O(klog⁡n).

Implementation
// In the name of god
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
typedef long long ll;
 
int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false), cin.tie(nullptr);
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while(t--) {
        int n, k;
        cin >> n >> k;
        int a[k]{}, p = 0;
        if(k&1)
            for(int i = 0; i < k; i++)
                a[i] = n;
        else {
            for(int i = 30; i >= 0; i--) {
                if(n >> i & 1) {
                    for(int j = 0; j < k; j++)
                        if(j != min(p, k-1))
                            a[j] += (1 << i);
                    if(p < k)
                        p++;
                } else
                    for(int j = 0; j < p/2*2; j++)
                        a[j] += (1 << i);
            }
        }
        for(int i = 0; i < k; i++)
            cout << a[i] << (i+1 == k? '\n':' ');
    }
    return 0;
}

2180D - Insolvable Disks

Idea: AmirrzwM, MohammadParsaElahimanesh — Preparation: AmirrzwM

Solution

Let x1,x2,…,xnx1,x2,…,xn be the sorted list of points, and let di (1≤i≤n−1)di (1≤i≤n−1) be the distance between consecutive points, i.e.

di=xi+1−xi.di=xi+1−xi.

We try to find the largest prefix of points that can be made connected.

Consider some valid choice of radii, and assume that the radius of the first disk is r1=xr1=x. In order for the second disk to be tangent to the first one, its radius must be

r2=d1−x.r2=d1−x.

Continuing in this way, the third disk must have radius

r3=d2−(d1−x)=d2−d1+x.r3=d2−(d1−x)=d2−d1+x.

In general, if we connect the first kk disks together, the radius of the ii-th disk (1≤i≤k)(1≤i≤k) must be

ri=di−1−di−2+di−3−⋯+(−1)id1+(−1)i+1x.ri=di−1−di−2+di−3−⋯+(−1)id1+(−1)i+1x.

For this configuration to be valid, we must have

0<ri<di.0<ri<di.

Substituting the expression for riri, we obtain

0<di−1−di−2+di−3−⋯+(−1)id1+(−1)i+1x<di.0<di−1−di−2+di−3−⋯+(−1)id1+(−1)i+1x<di.

For odd ii, this inequality becomes

0<di−1−di−2+di−3−⋯−d1+x<di,0<di−1−di−2+di−3−⋯−d1+x<di,

which is equivalent to

−di−1+di−2−di−3+⋯+d1<x<di−di−1+di−2−di−3+⋯+d1.−di−1+di−2−di−3+⋯+d1<x<di−di−1+di−2−di−3+⋯+d1.

For even ii, we obtain

0<di−1−di−2+di−3−⋯+d1−x<di,0<di−1−di−2+di−3−⋯+d1−x<di,

which is equivalent to

−di+di−1−di−2+di−3−⋯+d1<x<di−1−di−2+di−3−⋯+d1.−di+di−1−di−2+di−3−⋯+d1<x<di−1−di−2+di−3−⋯+d1.

Therefore, for each ii we obtain an interval of valid values for xx. By iterating over ii and maintaining the intersection of all these intervals, we can determine the first index where the intersection becomes empty. This index gives the largest prefix of disks that can be made connected.

After removing these disks, we can repeat the same process on the remaining ones. It can be shown that this procedure finds the correct answer and runs in O(n)O(n) time.

Why it is optimal.

For each index ii, let fifi be the rightmost position such that, for every jj with i≤j<fii≤j<fi, we can simultaneously make the jj-th disk tangent to the (j+1)(j+1)-th disk. Thus, ff is increasing sequence.

With this notation, the problem becomes selecting indices 1=i1<i2<⋯<ik1=i1<i2<⋯<ik such that

fit+1≥it+1(1≤t<k)andfik=n.fit+1≥it+1(1≤t<k)andfik=n.

By induction, we may assume it+1=fit+1it+1=fit+1, because choosing any smaller it+1≤fit+1it+1≤fit+1 cannot extend the reachable prefix beyond fitfit, while choosing exactly fit+1fit+1 is the earliest index that can potentially increase the farthest reachable position. Hence this greedy choice is optimal. Also, note that once we set it+1=fit+1it+1=fit+1, there are no additional constraints on the choice of rit+1rit+1 coming from earlier indices. In particular, any restriction on rit+1rit+1 can only be imposed by it+1+1it+1+1 (otherwise fitfit would not have been maximal by definition).

Implementation
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
 
using namespace std;
 
int main(){
    ios::sync_with_stdio(false) , cin.tie(0);
    int tt;
    cin >> tt;
    while(tt--){
        int n;
        cin >> n;
        int x[n];
        for(int i = 0 ; i < n ; i++) cin >> x[i];
        int ans = n-1;
        int l = 0 , r = +1e9+1;
        int cur = 0;
        int fst = 1;
        for(int i = 1 ; i < n ; i++){
            cur = (x[i] - x[i-1]) - cur;
            if(i % 2 == fst % 2){
                r = min(r , cur);
                if(i < n-1) l = max(l , cur - (x[i+1] - x[i]));
            }
            else{
                l = max(l , -cur);
                if(i < n-1) r = min(r , (x[i+1] - x[i]) - cur);
            }
            if(l >= r){
                ans--;
                l = 0 , r = +1e9+1;
                cur = 0;
                fst = i+1;
            }
        }
        cout << ans << endl;
    }
}

2180E - No Effect XOR

Idea: AmirrzwM — Preparation: MohammadParsaElahimanesh

Solution
Bitwise Approach

Step 0. Let us consider 00 as valid answer, for l=rl=r it's the only value, so assume l<rl<r. Also xor is a bijection, so if [l,r][l,r] maps to itself then its complement in WW (all non-negative integers) also maps to itself.

Step 1. Let's count the number of xx such that maps [0,n)[0,n) to itself. If n=2kn=2k then every 0≤x<n0≤x<n works. If 2k−1<n<2k2k−1<n<2k, then the (k−1)(k−1)-th bit of xx must be 00; otherwise it maps 2k−12k−1 into [0,n)[0,n), hence the answer equals the answer for [0,n−2k−1)[0,n−2k−1). Therefore the answer is simply the largest power of two dividing nn.

Step 2. Choose kk with 2k−1≤r<2k2k−1≤r<2k. If 2k−1≤l<r<2k2k−1≤l<r<2k, then every number in [l,r][l,r] has the (k−1)(k−1)-th bit equal to 11, so subtracting 2k−12k−1 from both ends just removes that common bit and does not change the answer; repeating this reduction, we may assume 0≤l<2k−1≤r<2k0≤l<2k−1≤r<2k.

Step 3. Work in the block [0,2k−1][0,2k−1] and define A=[0,l−1]A=[0,l−1], B=[l,r]B=[l,r], C=[r+1,2k−1]C=[r+1,2k−1]. We need B⊕x=BB⊕x=B. In this crossing case, every element of AA has (k−1)(k−1)-th bit 00 and every element of CC has (k−1)(k−1)-th bit 11, so xor by xx cannot send only part of AA to CC. Since xor is a bijection on the whole block, once BB maps to itself we must also map A∪CA∪C to itself, and there are only two possibilities:

  • Map A→AA→A, B→BB→B, C→CC→C. This is equivalent to simultaneously keeping the prefix A=[0,l)A=[0,l) invariant and also keeping the prefix A∪B=[0,r+1)A∪B=[0,r+1) invariant.

  • Map A→CA→C and C→AC→A, while still B→BB→B. This is possible only if |A|=|C||A|=|C|. Define x¯=x⊕(2k−1)x¯=x⊕(2k−1). Then for every xx that maps AA to AA, the value x¯x¯ maps AA to CC, and vice versa, since 2k−1=(11…1)22k−1=(11…1)2 acts like a mirror on [0,2k)[0,2k).

Linear Algebra Approach (Guessing a basis)

Let SS be the set of all valid xx (including 00). We claim that SS is closed under XOR. Assume x,y∈Sx,y∈S. By validity, for every i∈[l,r]i∈[l,r] we have

l≤i⊕x≤randl≤(i⊕x)⊕y≤r.l≤i⊕x≤randl≤(i⊕x)⊕y≤r.

But (i⊕x)⊕y=i⊕(x⊕y)(i⊕x)⊕y=i⊕(x⊕y), hence for all i∈[l,r]i∈[l,r],

l≤i⊕(x⊕y)≤r,l≤i⊕(x⊕y)≤r,

so x⊕y∈Sx⊕y∈S. Therefore SS forms a vector space over F2F2, and it is enough to find a basis.

Consider

fn(x):=x⊕(2n−1).fn(x):=x⊕(2n−1).

For 0≤x<2n0≤x<2n we have

x⊕(2n−1)=x⊕(111…1)2=2n−1−x,x⊕(2n−1)=x⊕(111…1)2=2n−1−x,

so fnfn acts like a mirror inside each block of length 2n2n. Motivated by this, we guess that basis elements are of the form (111…1)2(111…1)2, i.e. 2n−12n−1.

Now we determine for which nn we have 2n−1∈S2n−1∈S. Define the blocks

bk:=[k⋅2n,(k+1)⋅2n).bk:=[k⋅2n,(k+1)⋅2n).

If r−l+1>2nr−l+1>2n, then 2n−12n−1 is valid if and only if for every kk, the intersection [l,r]∩bk[l,r]∩bk is either empty or the whole block bkbk (equivalently, [l,r][l,r] never cuts a block partially), since fnfn preserves each block and reverses the order inside it.

We must also check one boundary case, where fnfn maps [l,r][l,r] to itself, which happens only if:

l⊕(2n−1)=r⇒l⊕r=2n−1l⊕(2n−1)=r⇒l⊕r=2n−1
⌊l2n⌋=⌊r2n⌋⌊l2n⌋=⌊r2n⌋
l≠k⋅2n (Counted before)l≠k⋅2n (Counted before)

In other words, ll and rr must be symmetric with respect to the midpoint of one block under the mirror fnfn.

Implementation
// In the name of god
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
typedef long long ll;
 
inline ll maxPower2Multiple(const ll &a) {return a&-a;}
inline ll prefixAnswer(const ll &a) {return maxPower2Multiple(a);}
inline bool isPerfect(const ll &a) {return a == maxPower2Multiple(a);}
 
int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false), cin.tie(nullptr);
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while(t--) {
        ll l, r;
        cin >> l >> r;
        ll both = 1LL<<60;
        while((both&l) == (both&r) && both > 0) {
            if(both&l) l -= both, r -= both;
            both >>= 1;
        }
        if(l and isPerfect(l+r+1))
            cout << 2LL*min(prefixAnswer(l), prefixAnswer(r+1LL))-1LL << '\n';
        else if(l)
            cout << min(prefixAnswer(l), prefixAnswer(r+1LL))-1LL << '\n';
        else
            cout << prefixAnswer(r+1LL)-1LL << '\n';
    }
    return 0;
}

2180F1 - Control Car (Easy Version)

Idea: MohammadParsaElahimanesh — Preparation: MohammadParsaElahimanesh, AmirrzwM

Solution

We solve the problem using dynamic programming.

A naive DP definition would be to let dp[i][j]dp[i][j] be the probability that a car, currently at cell (i,j)(i,j), eventually reaches the bottom-right corner of the grid. However, this state does not contain enough information to determine valid transitions, since the future movement of the car depends on the direction from which it entered the current cell and on the orientations of certain walls. Therefore, we need to add more information to our DP states.

We define the following DP states:

  • dp[i][j][0][0]dp[i][j][0][0]: the probability that the car reaches the bottom-right corner, given that it is currently at cell (i,j)(i,j), entered this cell from above, and the wall starting from the top-right corner of this cell is pointing downward.

  • dp[i][j][0][1]dp[i][j][0][1]: similar to dp[i][j][0][0]dp[i][j][0][0], but the wall starting from the top-right corner of the cell is \textbf{not} pointing downward.

  • dp[i][j][1][0]dp[i][j][1][0]: the probability that the car reaches the bottom-right corner, given that it is currently at cell (i,j)(i,j), entered this cell from the left, and the wall starting from the bottom-left corner of this cell is pointing to the right.

  • dp[i][j][1][1]dp[i][j][1][1]: similar to dp[i][j][1][0]dp[i][j][1][0], but the wall starting from the bottom-left corner of the cell is \textbf{not} pointing to the right.

We now describe how to compute the transitions.

Consider the state dp[i][j][0][0]dp[i][j][0][0]. In this case, the car is at cell (i,j)(i,j), it entered from above, and the top-right wall is pointing downward. Therefore, the car cannot move to cell (i,j+1)(i,j+1) and must move to cell (i+1,j)(i+1,j).

After moving to (i+1,j)(i+1,j), there is a probability of 316316 that the top-right wall of the new cell is pointing downward, and a probability of 3838 that it is not. Thus, we obtain

dp[i][j][0][0]=316dp[i+1][j][0][0]+38dp[i+1][j][0][1].dp[i][j][0][0]=316dp[i+1][j][0][0]+38dp[i+1][j][0][1].

Using similar arguments for the remaining states, we get the following transitions:

dp[i][j][0][1]=316dp[i+1][j][0][0]+38dp[i+1][j][0][1]+116dp[i][j+1][1][0]+516dp[i][j+1][1][1],dp[i][j][0][1]=316dp[i+1][j][0][0]+38dp[i+1][j][0][1]+116dp[i][j+1][1][0]+516dp[i][j+1][1][1],
dp[i][j][1][0]=316dp[i][j+1][1][0]+38dp[i][j+1][1][1],dp[i][j][1][0]=316dp[i][j+1][1][0]+38dp[i][j+1][1][1],
dp[i][j][1][1]=12dp[i+1][j][0][1]+14dp[i+1][j][0][0]+316dp[i][j+1][1][1].dp[i][j][1][1]=12dp[i+1][j][0][1]+14dp[i+1][j][0][0]+316dp[i][j+1][1][1].

By computing these DP states in reverse order of ii and jj, we can evaluate all states in O(nm)O(nm) time and obtain the final answer.

Implementation
// In the name of god
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
 
using namespace std;
typedef long long ll;
 
constexpr int T = 10000; 
constexpr int N = 5000;
constexpr ll mod = 1000000007;
constexpr ll inv2 = (mod+1)>>1;
constexpr ll inv4 = inv2>>1;
constexpr ll inv8 = inv4>>1;
constexpr ll inv16 = (inv8+mod)>>1;
ll dp[2][N+1][2][2];
vector<pair<int, int>> query[N+1];
ll ans[T];
 
inline ll Pow(ll a, ll b) {
    ll res = 1LL;
    b %= mod-1;
    while(b) {
        if(b&1) res = (res*a)%mod;
        a = (a*a)%mod; 
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return res;
}
 
int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false), cin.tie(nullptr);
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    for(int n, m, i = 0; i < t; i++) {
        cin >> n >> m;
        query[n].push_back({m, i});
    }
	for(int i = 0; i <= N; i++)
		dp[N&1][i][0][0] = dp[N&1][i][0][1] = dp[N&1][i][1][0] = dp[N&1][i][1][1] = 1;
    for(int i = N-1; i >= 0; i--) {
        int q = i&1, p = 1-(i&1);
		dp[q][N][0][0] = dp[q][N][0][1] = dp[q][N][1][0] = dp[q][N][1][1] = 1;
        for(int j = N-1; j >= 0; j--) {
            dp[q][j][0][0] = (dp[p][j][0][0]*3LL*inv16+dp[p][j][0][1]*3LL*inv8)%mod;
            dp[q][j][0][1] = ((dp[p][j][0][0]*3LL*inv16+dp[p][j][0][1]*3LL*inv8)%mod+
                                dp[q][j+1][1][0]*inv16+dp[q][j+1][1][1]*5LL*inv16)%mod;
            dp[q][j][1][0] = (dp[q][j+1][1][0]*3LL*inv16+dp[q][j+1][1][1]*3LL*inv8)%mod;
            dp[q][j][1][1] = (dp[p][j][0][0]*inv4+dp[p][j][0][1]*inv2+dp[q][j+1][1][1]*3LL*inv16)%mod;
        }
        for(auto [m, qu]: query[N-i]) {
            ll P = (dp[q][N-m][0][0]*inv4+dp[q][N-m][0][1]*3LL*inv4)%mod;
            ans[qu] = (1LL-P+mod)*Pow(4LL, 1LL*(N-i+1)*(m+1))%mod;
        }
    }
    for(int i = 0; i < t; i++)
        cout << ans[i] << '\n';
    return 0;
}

2180F2 - Control Car (Hard Version)

Idea: MohammadParsaElahimanesh — Preparation: MohammadParsaElahimanesh, AmirrzwM

Solution

Note that the jj-th layer of DP states (that is, dp[i][j]dp[i][j] for all valid ii) depends only on the (j−1)(j−1)-th layer and the jj-th layer itself. Moreover, all layers are computed using identical transition rules.

Let dp[j]dp[j] denote the vector consisting of all states dp[i][j]dp[i][j] for a fixed jj. Then there exists a constant transition matrix MM such that

dp[j]=M⋅dp[j−1].dp[j]=M⋅dp[j−1].

By computing Mm−1Mm−1 and multiplying it by the initial vector dp[1]dp[1], we obtain dp[m]dp[m], from which the final answer can be extracted.

This can be done in O(n3logm)O(n3log⁡m) time using binary exponentiation.

Implementation
// In the name of god
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
 
using namespace std;
typedef long long ll;
 
constexpr ll N = 50 + 1;
constexpr ll M = 1000000000000000LL;
constexpr ll mod = 1000000007;
constexpr ll inv2 = (mod+1)>>1;
constexpr ll inv4 = inv2>>1;
constexpr ll inv8 = inv4>>1;
constexpr ll inv16 = (inv8+mod)>>1;
ll n, m;
 
struct DP {
    ll dp[N][2][2][N][2][2] = {};
};
 
inline DP upd(const DP& l, const DP& r) {
    DP lr;
    for(int li = 0; li <= n; li++)
        for(int lh = 0; lh < 2; lh++)
            for(int lk = 0; lk < 2; lk++)
                for(int ri = li; ri <= n; ri++)
                    for(int rh = 0; rh < 2; rh++)
                        for(int rk = 0; rk < 2; rk++) 
                            for(int mi = li; mi <= ri; mi++) {
                                for(int mh = 0; mh < 2; mh++)
                                    for(int mk = 0; mk < 2; mk++)
                                        lr.dp[li][lh][lk][ri][rh][rk] += l.dp[li][lh][lk][mi][mh][mk]*r.dp[mi][mh][mk][ri][rh][rk];
                                lr.dp[li][lh][lk][ri][rh][rk] %= mod;
                            }
    return lr;
}
 
inline DP Identical() {
    DP I;
    for(int i = 0; i < n; i++)
        for(int j = 0; j < 2; j++)
            for(int k = 0; k < 2; k++)
                I.dp[i][j][k][i][j][k] = 1;
    return I;
}
 
inline DP Pow(DP a, ll b) {
    DP res = Identical();
    while(b) {
        if(b&1) res = upd(res, a);
        a = upd(a, a);
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return res;
}
 
inline ll Pow(ll a, ll b) {
    ll res = 1LL;
    while(b) {
        if(b&1) res = res*a%mod;
        a = a*a%mod;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return res;
}
 
int main() 
{
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while(t--) {
        cin >> n >> m;
        DP col;
    	col.dp[n][0][0][n][0][0] = col.dp[n][0][1][n][0][1] = col.dp[n][1][0][n][1][0] = col.dp[n][1][1][n][1][1] = 1;
        for(int i = (int)n-1; i >= 0; i--) {
            for(int j = i+1; j <= n; j++)
                for(int k = 0; k < 2; k++)
                    for(int l = 0; l < 2; l++) {
                        col.dp[i][0][1][j][k][l] = col.dp[i][0][0][j][k][l] = (col.dp[i+1][0][0][j][k][l]*3LL*inv16+col.dp[i+1][0][1][j][k][l]*3LL*inv8)%mod;
                        col.dp[i][1][1][j][k][l] = (col.dp[i+1][0][0][j][k][l]*inv4+col.dp[i+1][0][1][j][k][l]*inv2)%mod;
                    }
            col.dp[i][0][1][i][1][0] = inv16;
            col.dp[i][0][1][i][1][1] = 5LL*inv16%mod;
            col.dp[i][1][0][i][1][0] = 3LL*inv16%mod;
            col.dp[i][1][0][i][1][1] = 3LL*inv8%mod;
            col.dp[i][1][1][i][1][1] = 3LL*inv16; 
        }
        DP dpnm = Pow(col, m);
        ll ans = 0;
        for(int i = 0; i <= n; i++)
            for(int j = 0; j < 2; j++)
                for(int k = 0; k < 2; k++)
                    ans = (ans+dpnm.dp[0][0][0][i][j][k]*inv4+dpnm.dp[0][0][1][i][j][k]*3LL*inv4)%mod;
        cout << (1LL-ans+mod)*Pow(4LL, 1LL*(n+1LL)*(m+1LL))%mod << '\n';
    }
    return 0;
}

2180G - Balance

Idea: MohammadParsaElahimanesh, AmirrzwM, Shayan — Preparation: AmirrzwM

Solution

First, note that due to the structure of the queries, the array aa is always a palindrome.

Let nn be the current length of the array. Define ci,m,kci,m,k as the number of subsequences of aa of length mm in which aiai appears as the kk-th element. Then the total sum of balances over all non-empty subsequences is equal to

∑ni=1∑nm=1∑mk=1ai⋅ci,m,k⋅km.∑i=1n∑m=1n∑k=1mai⋅ci,m,k⋅km.

By symmetry of the palindrome, we have

ci,m,k=cn−i+1,m,m−k+1.ci,m,k=cn−i+1,m,m−k+1.

Using this symmetry, we can write

2⋅balance=∑i,m,kai⋅ci,m,k⋅km+∑i,m,kan−i+1⋅cn−i+1,m,m−k+1⋅m−k+1m.2⋅balance=∑i,m,kai⋅ci,m,k⋅km+∑i,m,kan−i+1⋅cn−i+1,m,m−k+1⋅m−k+1m.

After rearranging terms, this implies that the total balance can be decomposed into two parts:

  • the sum of all elements over all non-empty subsequences,
  • plus the sum of averages of all non-empty subsequences.

The first part is straightforward. Each element aiai appears in exactly 2n−12n−1 non-empty subsequences, hence

∑all subsequences∑ai=2n−1∑ni=1ai.∑all subsequences∑ai=2n−1∑i=1nai.

For the second part, note that aiai contributes to subsequences of length kk exactly (n−1k−1)(n−1k−1) times, and in such subsequences its coefficient in the average is 1k1k. Therefore,

∑ni=1∑nk=1ai⋅(n−1k−1)k=∑ni=1ai⋅∑nk=1(n−1k−1)k.∑i=1n∑k=1nai⋅(n−1k−1)k=∑i=1nai⋅∑k=1n(n−1k−1)k.

It is easy to verify that

∑nk=1(n−1k−1)k=2n−1n.∑k=1n(n−1k−1)k=2n−1n.

Hence the second part equals

2n−1n∑ni=1ai.2n−1n∑i=1nai.

Combining both parts, if we denote

S=∑ni=1ai,S=∑i=1nai,

the answer to a type 33 query is

2n−1⋅S+2n−1n⋅S.2n−1⋅S+2n−1n⋅S.

Thus, it is sufficient to maintain the values of nn and SS. Since all computations are done modulo 109+7109+7, we maintain:

  • Smod(109+7)Smod(109+7),
  • nmod(109+7)nmod(109+7),
  • nmod(109+6)nmod(109+6) (for computing powers of 22).

Updating SS and nn for queries of type 22 is trivial. Updating nn for queries of type 11 is also straightforward. The only non-trivial part is determining which element is removed in a type 11 query in order to update SS.

To handle this, consider a type 22 query inserting a value xx. There are two cases.

  • If the current length of aa is odd (2n+12n+1), after insertion the array becomes
[x,a1,x,…,x,an+1,x,…,x,a2n+1,x].[x,a1,x,…,x,an+1,x,…,x,a2n+1,x].

The first time the middle element is removed, it is the original middle element of aa. The next two times, the removed element is xx. After that, the situation is equivalent to applying the same process to the array obtained by removing the middle element of aa.

  • If the current length of aa is even (2n2n), after insertion the array becomes
[x,a1,x,…,an,x,an+1,x,…,a2n,x].[x,a1,x,…,an,x,an+1,x,…,a2n,x].

The first removed middle element is xx, the second one is the original middle element of aa (namely an+1an+1), and afterwards the situation again reduces to the same process on the array with its middle element removed.

Using this observation, we maintain a list of all values added by type 22 queries, in order. For each such value we store an integer state in 0,1,2,30,1,2,3. When a new value xx is inserted:

  • if the current length is odd, we assign it state 33,
  • otherwise, we assign it state 11.

When processing a type 11 query, we traverse this list from the end. For each element:

  • if its state is 00 or 11, this element is the current middle element; we update its state to (state+1)mod4(state+1)mod4 and return its value,
  • otherwise, we only update its state to (state+1)mod4(state+1)mod4 and continue.

This procedure finds the middle element in O(1)O(1) amortized time per query.

Therefore, the overall time complexity of the solution is O(qlogM)O(qlog⁡M).

Implementation
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
 
using namespace std;
 
long long pw(long long a , long long b , long long mod){
  long long ret = 1;
  long long mul = a;
  while(b > 0){
	if(b&1)
	  ret = (ret * mul) % mod;
	mul = (mul * mul) % mod;
	b >>= 1;
  }
  return ret;
}
 
const int mod = 1e9 + 7;
 
int q;
 
int sum = 0 , lenm = 0 , lenm1 = 0 , len2 = 0;
 
vector<pair<int,int>> adds; // stay stay pass pass
 
int get(int id , int del = 1){
    assert(id >= 0);
    if(adds[id].second < 2){
        if(del) adds[id].second = (adds[id].second + 1) & 3;
        return adds[id].first;
    }
    else{
        if(del) adds[id].second = (adds[id].second + 1) & 3;
        return get(id-1 , del);
    }
}
 
int main() 
{
    ios::sync_with_stdio(false) , cin.tie(0);
    int tt;
    cin >> tt;
    while(tt--){
        cin >> q;
        sum = 0 , lenm = 0 , lenm1 = 0 , len2 = 0;
        adds.clear();
        while(q--){
            int tp;
            cin >> tp;
            if(tp == 1){
                sum = (sum - get(adds.size()-1 , 1) + mod) % mod;
                lenm = (lenm - 1 + mod) % mod;
                lenm1 = (lenm1 - 1 + (mod-1)) % (mod-1);
                len2 ^= 1;
            }
            else if(tp == 2){
                int x;
                cin >> x;
                if(len2 == 1) adds.push_back({x , 3});
                else adds.push_back({x , 1});
                sum = (sum + 1ll * (lenm + 1) * x) % mod;
                lenm = (2 * lenm + 1) % mod;
                lenm1 = (2 * lenm1 + 1) % (mod-1);
                len2 = 1;
            }
            else if(tp == 3){
                int ans = (pw(2 , (lenm1 - 1 + (mod-1)) % (mod-1) , mod) + 1ll * (pw(2 , lenm1 , mod) - 1) * pw(lenm , mod-2 , mod)) % mod;
                ans = (1ll * ans * sum) % mod;
                ans = (1ll * ans * pw(2 , mod-2 , mod)) % mod;
                cout << ans << endl;
            }
        }
    }
    return 0;
}

2180H1 - Bug Is Feature (Unconditional Version)

Idea: Akulyat — Preparation: MohammadParsaElahimanesh

Solution

Step 1: reduce to independent series.

First, we can split the whole match into independent series (ai,bi,ci,[li,ri])(ai,bi,ci,[li,ri]). Since moves never mix two different games, by the Sprague-Grundy theorem we can compute the Grundy number contributed by each series and then solve the question.

Step 2: normalize one game.

Fix one game with upper bound xx. Shift everything by aa:

(a,b,c,x) ⟶ (0, b−a, c−a, x−a).(a,b,c,x) ⟶ (0, b−a, c−a, x−a).

Because a,b,ca,b,c are an arithmetic progression, we have c−a=2(b−a)c−a=2(b−a). Put d=b−ad=b−a and y=x−ay=x−a, so the state starts as

(0, d, 2d, y).(0, d, 2d, y).

Any move keeps the three numbers in an arithmetic progression (after reordering). In this normalized form, every legal move transforms (0, d, 2d, y)(0, d, 2d, y) into exactly one of the following forms: - (d, 2d, 3d, y)(d, 2d, 3d, y), - (0, 2d, 4d, y)(0, 2d, 4d, y), - (d, 3d2, 2d, y)(d, 3d2, 2d, y) when dd is even.

Step 3: parameters (t,d)(t,d) and then (q,k)(q,k).

It is convenient to measure the remaining room under yy:

t=y−2d.t=y−2d.

Then the game state is represented by (t,d)(t,d).

Write

d=2k⋅D,D odd,d=2k⋅D,D odd,

and define

q=⌊tD⌋.q=⌊tD⌋.

After this, the game depends only on (q,k)(q,k). We denote its Grundy number by g(q,k)g(q,k).

Step 4: large ranges collapse to a few ranges of qq.

In a fixed series, dd is fixed, hence kk and DD are fixed. As xx runs through [li,ri][li,ri], the value t=x−cit=x−ci runs through an interval [L,R][L,R] where L=li−ciL=li−ci and R=ri−ciR=ri−ci, and we look at

q=⌊tD⌋.q=⌊tD⌋.

This qq stays constant on blocks of consecutive DD values, and the full blocks have length DD. Since DD is odd, the contribution of the whole interval t∈[L,R]t∈[L,R] can be reduced to:

  • one consecutive interval of qq values corresponding to full blocks,

  • at most two boundary blocks (the beginning and the end), treated separately.

Step 5: recursion for g(q,k)g(q,k).

From the move description on (q,k)(q,k), we can write

g(q,k)=mex(g(q−2k,k), g(q,k−1), g(q−2k+1,k+1)).g(q,k)=mex(g(q−2k,k), g(q,k−1), g(q−2k+1,k+1)).

Two facts make it easy to work with:

  • the third term is never needed, so g(q,k)=mex(g(q−2k,k), g(q,k−1))g(q,k)=mex(g(q−2k,k), g(q,k−1)).

  • for fixed kk, the sequence g(0,k),g(1,k),…g(0,k),g(1,k),… is periodic with period 2k+12k+1.

Using the first fact, we get a simple split:

  • if q<2kq<2k, then g(q−2k,k)g(q−2k,k) does not exist, so g(q,k)=mex(g(q,k−1))g(q,k)=mex(g(q,k−1)).
  • if q≥2kq≥2k, then g(q,k)=mex(g(q,k−1), g(q−2k,k))g(q,k)=mex(g(q,k−1), g(q−2k,k)).

Step 6: reduce interval XOR to prefix XOR.

In each series we need

g(L,k)⊕g(L+1,k)⊕⋯⊕g(R,k).g(L,k)⊕g(L+1,k)⊕⋯⊕g(R,k).

Define the prefix XOR

f(u,k)=g(0,k)⊕g(1,k)⊕⋯⊕g(u,k).f(u,k)=g(0,k)⊕g(1,k)⊕⋯⊕g(u,k).

Then

g(l,k)⊕⋯⊕g(r,k)=f(r,k)⊕f(l−1,k).g(l,k)⊕⋯⊕g(r,k)=f(r,k)⊕f(l−1,k).

Step 7: compute f(u,k)f(u,k) as a small tuple.

We save f(u,k)f(u,k) as a tuple

(p0,p1,p2),(p0,p1,p2),

where pi=1pi=1 means the value ii has an odd contribution in

f(u,k)=g(0,k)⊕g(1,k)⊕⋯⊕g(u,k).f(u,k)=g(0,k)⊕g(1,k)⊕⋯⊕g(u,k).

In other words, pipi tells whether ii appears an odd number of times among g(0,k),…,g(u,k)g(0,k),…,g(u,k).

We first precompute the XOR over one full period

f(2k+1−1,k).f(2k+1−1,k).

Since the period is 2k+12k+1, two full consecutive periods cancel in XOR, so the XOR over length 2k+22k+2 becomes (0,0,0)(0,0,0). Therefore, for a general uu we reduce it to the remainder inside a single period, and then compute it by recursion on kk from the formulas in Step 5. This makes computing f(u,k)f(u,k) fast, in O(k)=O(logu)O(k)=O(log⁡u) time.

Implementation
// In the name of God
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;
#pragma GCC optimize("O3,unroll-loops,no-stack-protector")
#pragma GCC target("avx2,bmi,bmi2,lzcnt,popcnt")
typedef long long ll;
 
inline ll lg2(ll x){return 63 - __builtin_clzll(x);}
constexpr inline ll period(ll k) {return 1LL<<(k+1);}
constexpr inline ll half_period(ll k) {return 1LL<<k;}
inline ll equivalent(ll x, ll k) {return x & (period(k)-1);}
inline bool count_period_parity(ll x, ll k) {return (x >> (k+1)) & 1;}
 
struct node {
    int u;
    node(bool zero, bool one, bool two) {
        u = ((int) zero << 0) + ((int) one << 1) + ((int) two << 2);
    }
    node(int x) {
        u = x;
    }
    bool has(int i) const {return u & (1<<i);}
    node Mex() const {
        return node(has(1) ^ has(2), has(0), false);
    }
    node MexMex() const {
        return node(false, has(2), has(0) ^ has(1));
    }
    node operator^(const node other) const {
        return node(u ^ other.u);
    }
    void operator^=(const node other) {
        u ^= other.u;
    }
    int get() const {
        return (u >> 1);
    }
};
 
const node full_period(true, true, false);
const node full_period_mex = full_period.Mex();
const node zero_node(false, false, false);
const node zero(true, false, false), one(false, true, false), two(false, false, true);
 
node grundy(ll x, ll k) {
    if(k)
        return equivalent(x, k) < half_period(k)? grundy(x, k-1).Mex() : grundy(x, k-1).MexMex();
    return x&1? one : zero;
}
 
node prefix_grundy(ll r, ll k) {
    node period_effect = (count_period_parity(r, k)? full_period : zero_node);
    r = equivalent(r, k);
    if(k == 0)
        return (r? full_period : zero) ^ period_effect;
    if(r < half_period(k))
        return prefix_grundy(r, k-1).Mex() ^ period_effect;
    return prefix_grundy(r - half_period(k), k-1).MexMex() ^ full_period_mex ^ period_effect;
}
 
inline node grundy(ll l, ll r, ll k) {
    return (l? prefix_grundy(l-1, k) : zero_node) ^ prefix_grundy(r, k);
}
 
int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false), cin.tie(nullptr);
    int n;
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while(t--) {
        cin >> n;
        node ans(0);
        for(ll a, b, c, l, r, lp, rp, d, k, i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            cin >> a >> b >> c >> l >> r;
            b -= a, c -= a, l -= a, r -= a, a = 0;
            l -= c, r -= c;
            k = lg2(b & -b), d = b >> k;
            if(l / d == r / d) {
                if((r - l + 1) & 1)
                    ans ^= grundy(l / d, k);
            } else {
                lp = (l+d-1) / d, rp = r / d;
                if(lp < rp) ans ^= grundy(lp, rp-1, k);
                if((lp*d-l)&1) ans ^= grundy(l / d, k);
                if((r-rp*d+1)&1) ans ^= grundy(r / d, k);
            }
        }
        cout << (ans.get()? "Bug" : "Feature") << '\n';
    }
    return 0;
}
// Thank God

2180H2 - Bug Is Feature (Conditional Version)

Idea: MohammadParsaElahimanesh, AmirrzwM, Shayan — Preparation: MohammadParsaElahimanesh

Solution

Step 1: Game Transformation.

Convert each game with ai<bi<ci≤xai<bi<ci≤x to analyze 0<1<2≤x′0<1<2≤x′ where:

x′=⌊x−aidi⌋,di=bi−aix′=⌊x−aidi⌋,di=bi−ai

The interval [li,ri][li,ri] transforms to

[Li,Ri]=[⌊li−aidi⌋,⌊ri−aidi⌋].[Li,Ri]=[⌊li−aidi⌋,⌊ri−aidi⌋].

Note that each x′∈[Li,Ri]x′∈[Li,Ri] corresponds to multiple original xx values (the number of originals can change near the interval endpoints). From state x′x′, a player can move either to x′−1x′−1 or to ⌊x′2⌋⌊x′2⌋. The game ends when x′=2x′=2, and the player who makes the last move wins.

Step 2: Grundy Numbers.

Define g(x)g(x) for state 0<1<2≤x0<1<2≤x:

  • Base cases:
g(2)=0,g(3)=1g(2)=0,g(3)=1
  • Recursive rule (x≥4x≥4):
g(x)=MEX(g(x−1), g(⌊x2⌋))g(x)=MEX(g(x−1), g(⌊x2⌋))

  • Key properties (proven by induction):

    • g(x)=0g(x)=0 ⟺⟺ xx has odd trailing zeros in binary
    • Non-zero g(x)g(x) alternate 1↔21↔2 sequentially

Step 3: Counting Grundy Values.

For any interval [l,r][l,r]:

  • Count numbers with g(x)=0g(x)=0:

    • Define C(y)=C(y)= count of x≤y∣g(x)=0x≤y∣g(x)=0
    • Loop through bits to compute C(y)C(y) in O(logy)O(log⁡y)
    • Zeros in [l,r][l,r]: C(r)−C(l−1)C(r)−C(l−1)

  • Count 11's and 22's similarly using C(y)C(y) and alternation of 11 and 22.

Step 4: Final Computation.

For each transformed interval [Li,Ri][Li,Ri]:

  • Compute XOR of all g(x)g(x) in [Li,Ri][Li,Ri]
  • Aggregate XOR across all nn queries
  • Total XOR ≠0⟹≠0⟹ Bug wins; else Feature wins
Implementation
// In the name of God
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;
typedef long long ll;
 
inline ll count_zeros(ll x) {
    int u = (x&2)>>1;
    ll cnt = u;
    while (x>>=2) {
        u = (x&2)>>1;
        cnt += x+u;
    }
    return cnt;
}
 
// normal grundy function
/*
inline int grundy(ll x) {
    if((__builtin_ctzll(x)&1) == 1) return 0;
    return 1+((x-count_zeros(x))&1);
}
*/
 
// magical function
inline int grundy(ll x) {
    if((__builtin_ctzll(x)&1) == 1) return 0;
    return 1+(__builtin_popcountll(x)&1);
}
 
inline int grundy(ll l, ll r) {
    ll zel = count_zeros(l-1), zer = count_zeros(r);
    ll onl = (l-1-zel)/2, onr = (r-zer)/2;
    ll twl = (l-2-zel)/2, twr = (r-1-zer)/2;
    return ((onr-onl)&1)^(((twr-twl)&1)<<1);
}
 
int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false), cin.tie(0);
    int n, ans;
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while(t--) {
        cin >> n;
        ans = 0;
        for(ll a, b, c, l, r, lp, rp, i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            cin >> a >> b >> c >> l >> r;
            b -= a, c -= a, l -= a, r -= a, a = 0;
            if(l / b == r / b) {
                if((r - l + 1)&1)
                    ans ^= grundy(l / b);
            } else {
                lp = (l+b-1)/b, rp = r/b;
                if(b&1) ans ^= grundy(lp, rp-1);
                if((lp*b-l)&1)ans ^= grundy(l/b);
                if((r-rp*b+1)&1)ans ^= grundy(rp);
            }
        }
        cout << (ans?"Bug":"Feature") << '\n';
    }
    return 0;
}
Tutorial of Codeforces Global Round 31 (Div. 1 + Div. 2)
editorial
  • -571
  • MohammadParsaElahimanesh
  • 3 months ago
  • 142

      3 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      if it be a larger one and a smaller one(such as break 2 times, one ends at 9, one ends at 8), then we see that
      the first one has answer 7 at 1-10 , and we can make r_10->0, it will be 7+(11-n)
      the second one has answer 6 at 1-9 , so it will be 6+(10-n)<=6+(10-11)+(11-n)<=7+(11-n)
      proof ends.
      note that (a-b) means the answer from a to b(e.t:(1-2)=1, (5-6)=0 or 1, (1-n)=the answer to output)

      -fft-

      -fft-

      +3

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        tryingoutcp
        3 months ago, show (+1) #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        tryingoutcp
        3 months ago, hide # ^ |
         
        +22

        I have understood it intuitively like this:

        We are building the numbers from the highest bit to the lowest. The restriction that ai<nai<n is annoying, but basically only matters for aiai's where the bits don't match with nn. So each time we're forced to place a 00, we place it for aiai's where the bits match with nn so far, this is exactly what the editorial is calling as "tight". Once aiai no longer matches with nn, we can place 1s even where nn has the bit as 0.

        The issue with OP's solution is the following: you're only placing the extra 0s at the last 2 numbers. The "give 1 to y and 0 to x" is the right idea, so that from now on we can add 1s to both x and y after that point, but what about the remaining k−2k−2 numbers? For cases where the bit is set to 1 in nn but we're forced to add a 0, adding 0 to these k−2k−2 numbers would be useful too. But we're fixing them as 1 in the wrong solution and always giving the 0 to y which is wasteful in a sense. It would be more useful if the 0 is given somewhere else, so that later on we can set 1 even where n is set as 0.

        Hence keeping as many numbers as loose is useful.

        Also in general, when you have a greedy strategy, your question shouldn't be "why doesn't it work". Do you have a proof, or a clear reasoning as to why it's correct? It's on the solver to prove the greedy idea. Realistically you can't formally prove ideas in a contest, but strong reasoning must be there as to why it's correct.

        Here the solution with loose and tight aiai's works because it is optimal to keep as many bits set as possible when moving from MSB to LSB. If we keep a 0 where we could have kept a 1, the only gain is in the rest of the bits of that number, which is not good enough to be better than the loss we got from a higher bit. And once we follow this strategy, we also want to keep as many numbers "loose" as possible.

        This in my opinion is reasoning you can realistically come up with in a contest as to why the editorial's greedy idea is correct. (Not that it's easy to come up with this, I myself didn't lol)

        → Reply

    ← Rev. 2 →  
    0

    Taking k-2 n's is not optimal,starting from MSB for each set bit give k-1 elements bit 1 and one number bit 0 at that position for the next set bit choose a different element to give bit 0 this way when we have a 0 we can choose to give even number of 1's to some of the elements in which case the sum of numbers will be larger. Basically you are only allocating two 1's (in x and y) for the unset bit in n but it is possible that more than two 1's can be allocated.

    0

    Well, I tried the same approach in the contest, and I was as confused as you about how it didn't work until someone showed me this: (I'm going to give the examples in binary so that it looks immediately correct)

    • n = 0b101101001101101101
    • k = 8
    • ans =
    • [0b001111111111111111,
    • 0b100111111111111111,
    • 0b101001111111111111,
    • 0b101100111111111111,
    • 0b101101000111111111,
    • 0b101101001011111111,
    • 0b101101001100111111,
    • 0b101101001101010010]

    I see that you tried exploiting the second 1 that you encounter in n to obtain a greater sum. That was my idea, too. But why not try to exploit the 1's as much as possible? So, lesson I learned: think a little deeper, and get a stronger reasoning.

  • »
    »
    accord
    3 months ago, show (+7) #
    »
    »
    accord
    3 months ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 5 →  
    +20

    I tried to write down my approach to Problem C, it is a bit hard to explain in words.

    The odd case is trivial.

    For the even case, observe that in order for XOR-sum to equal NN, at every jj-th bit where NN is 1, in the answer, K−1K−1 numbers must have the jj-th bit set to 1, and one must be a 0. Let's call such bit positions 1-bits.

    For the bits where NN is 0, ideally we will want to set all KK numbers of the output to 1's, but this is not always possible because our numbers in the output might become larger than NN.

    So, we can take advantage of the fact that at every 1-bit of NN, we can take an opportunity to set a particular bit to 0, and from then onward this number will be strictly smaller than NN, so further 0 bits can be turned on to maximize the sum, in these numbers. (Note that only even number of such 0 must be flipped on, though)

    Key is to choose different number each time to turn off a particular 1-bit, and not only one number, which is the trap.

    Time Complexity. O(K+(log N)2)O(K+(log N)2).

    → Reply

        can you explain why

        (Note that only even number of such 0 must be flipped on, though)

        • »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          accord
          3 months ago, show #
          »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          accord
          3 months ago, hide # ^ |
           
          0

          For the 0-bit positions, we want the final XOR-sum to be equal to 00 at those positions. Thus at each such bit position in the output numbers, we should flip an even number of them (as many as we can without the numbers exceeding NN, of course).

          → Reply

+8

I would like to share a solution for pE using the segment tree insertion function.

Similar to observations made in other solutions, we can see that for the same high-bit, if the count of 0s does not equal the count of 1s, swapping is definitely not possible. Conversely, if they are equal, swapping has no impact.

We can use a divide-and-conquer approach to maintain the counts of 0s and 1s.Specifically, if the range [l,r] covers the entire interval, the feasibility of this bit remains unaffected because the counts of 0s and 1s in this interval are identical. Therefore, we can ignore the coverage relationship—much like a segment tree. We can maintain the solution for this problem using a similar method. The code is as follows:

https://codeforces.com/contest/2180/submission/354220593

→ Reply

However, their number is different, so that is wrong.

JeanBombeur
3 months ago, show (+1) #
»
JeanBombeur
3 months ago, hide # |
 
+16

If you're like me and keep on misreading statements, you might be interested in the variation of problem F where walls have infinite length (in one direction). I believe I found a solution :

Spoiler

We calculate dp[i][j] = number of ways to orient the top-left corner of the grid such that the car is falling in column j on row i. We orient only the top-left corner (first j columns of first i rows) because those are the only points for which there will not be any additional constraint.

The transitions are : the car falls on row i at position k, and falls to row i + 1 at position j >= k. We can figure out how many orientations we get (points on rows < i + 1 and column between k and j should point up or right, and on row i + 1, the first j points should point left or down (or up for column 0), the j-th point should definetely point left if j != k, and the rest will be fixed by later transitions.

With that, we can get the number of ways to make the car fall off the grid at row n and column j, and multiply by the points that we have not yet fixed.

As for the number of ways to make the car exit the grid to the right, we can count it on each row : if the car is falling on row i at column j, and exits the grid to the right on row i, then all points on rows < i + 1 and columns >= j should point up or right, and there should be no holes on row i from column j onward and no walls going up. So we have 4∗3m4∗3m possibilities, from which we must substract ways that leave a hole : there are 3∗2m3∗2m ways to leave a hole at column j, and 3∗2m−13∗2m−1 ways to leave the first hole at column k>jk>j (again by looking at constraints), so we can find the answer.

Finally we get an O(nm2)O(nm2) dp which can easily be translated to an O(nm)O(nm) dp with some prefix sums, since transitions are very similar

I believe this code should work (I checked (n,m)=(1,1),(1,2),(2,1)(n,m)=(1,1),(1,2),(2,1))

Spoiler

void solve() {
    int n, m; cin >> n >> m;


    vector <mint> dp(m);
    dp[0] = 1;
    
    //  dp[i] = nb orientations of top-left corner with car falling in position i
    
    mint out = 0;
    
    rep (i, 0, n) {
        vector <mint> nv(m);
        rep (j, 0, m) {
            rep (k, 0, j + 1) {
                //  adding row i and k -> j columns
                nv[j] += dp[k] * 3 * mint(2).exp(j - (k < j)) * mint(2).exp((j - k) * i);
            }
            
            //  numbers of ways to exit to the right on this row
            mint add = dp[j] * mint(2).exp((m - j) * i) * (4 * mint(3).exp(m) - 3 * mint(2).exp(m) - (m - j - 1) * 3 * mint(2).exp(m - 1));
            
            //  adding top and bottom rows
            out += add * mint(4 * mint(3).exp(m)).exp(n - i);
        }
        dp.swap(nv);
    }
    
    //  adding top row and right columns
    rep (i, 0, m)
        out += dp[i] * mint(3).exp(i) * mint(4).exp(m + 1 - i) * mint(3).exp((m - i) * n);
    
    cout << mint(4).exp((n + 1) * (m + 1)) - out << '\n';
    
    return;
}
→ Reply

How to prove greedy in D?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    vasuganesha1
    3 months ago, show (+4) #
    »
    »
    vasuganesha1
    3 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +3

    If a circle with center at i th position is connected to the circle with center at the (i + 1)th position then let it be represented by 1.

    So, A binary string will be formed of length n — 1.

    Example 0011100111

    So the string ith character is representing, if the circle at center i is connected to the circle at center i + 1.

    So our greedy statergy to start from the ith position and go untill we are able to form a

    continuous line of connected cycle.

    so let the ith character be first positon where the optimal and greedy statergy differs

    1111011(Greedy) 1110111(Optimal)

    Now since character at i + 2 depending only on the i +1 character not on ith character so we can create optimal solution starting from i + 2 character without depending on first i characters of the greedy

    Sorry for the bad explanation but hope you get the idea.

    → Reply

      +34

      Making the problem harder with ridiculous limits is so disgusting. There's absolutely no reason to give N=5000. Look at the solutions that didn't toggle, they use 400MB or 500MB, so just give 1024MB. This has to be the worst limit I've seen in cf after that 120^5 problem. Also for gevak, you can save 4^0~4^m, 4^(m*i) (i<=n) and make the memory smaller(Which I couldn't come up during the contest and failed to reduce the memory at 600MB)

        3 months ago, show (+1) #

        »
        gevak
        3 months ago, hide # ^ |
        ← Rev. 2 →  
        +8

        Interesting idea about memorization 4^0~4^m, 4^(m*i) (i<=n)!

        Finally managed to get AC, storing all n*n*2*2 states and all n*n powers of 4: https://codeforces.com/contest/2180/submission/354268056

        int dp[5001][5001][2][2] stores answer for all states, which takes 400MB. int pow4[5001 * 5001] stores all powers of 4, which takes 100MB.

        Thus 500MB in total. And this iterative version takes 3000ms time. Recursive version takes 6000ms, which is close, but just a bit over TL.

        Interesting problem, but very frustrating N <= 5000 limit.

    +8

    Btw, declaring an array of 4n24n2 is possible, if you use the int type. Together with a precalculated power array, this gives 5n2⋅4byte≈400MB5n2⋅4byte≈400MB. Recursion is not a good idea anyway for O(n2)O(n2) with n=5000n=5000. (In the end because I needed 6n26n2 space I did do space-saving style DP so I didn't need the 4n24n2 of storage). I do agree that adjusting limits to either side would have been better. Lower, such that for example n2n2 integers does not fit, makes sure that you have to do some memory optimization. Higher, just makes sure not too many approaches are on the edge of memory limit.

Dark_Knyght
3 months ago, show #
»
Dark_Knyght
3 months ago, hide # |
 
0

Here is my approach for C

for odd k , just print n 'k' times

for even k ,

My idea was to try to bring in as much numbers(out of those k) in that category 'which will have 1 at the bit when n has a 0 at that bit' and as soon as possible .

starting from the highest set bit in n, let the bit be 'b': if b is 1 :
we need to have odd number of 1's (in those k numbers combined) at the b th bit , and so obviously to maximise the sum, we would want k-1 numbers to be set 1 at this bit. so make the 1st number of the list have a 0 bit here and rest all 1 bit. Next time we encounter a 1 in n , we make the second number of the list 0 here at this bit and rest all 1 . So after encountering 'k' 1's in n , we would have tried out all k numbers. Now , all the k numbers have been made < n .and in future whenever we encounter a 1 in n , doesn't matter you can make any of the k numbers 0 randomly .

And meanwhile in this process, whenever we encounter a 0 in n , we make the first 2*(num/2) numbers of the list to be 1 here. (Where num is the count of numbers which have been made < n so far) . Once num hits k , we can make all the numbers to be 1 here without violating [0,n] condition.

→ Reply

toba
3 months ago, show (+2) #
»
toba
3 months ago, hide # |
 
+4

In D, when we remove the disks from a prefix, the radius of the last center in the prefix will still affect the next set.

How do we guarantee that our choice of the radius of the first center in the second set will not cause it to overlap with the circle from the previous set?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    gabriel
    3 months ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    gabriel
    3 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +6

    Say the ith circle is the one that wasn't able to connect to the prefix. This means that even if it had its maximum possible radius (i.e., min(x[i+1]-x[i], x[i]-x[i-1]) it couldn't reach the (i-1)th circle. This also means that any radius you choose futurely (when you start a new "prefix component" at i) won't be affected by the (i-1)th radius, because it will never happen that the ith and (i-1)th circles touch.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      toba
      3 months ago, show #
      »
      »
      »
      toba
      3 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      +3

      → Reply

qiuzx

+20

For G, you would end up with something /(L−1)/(L−1) if you didn't realize that the sequence is palindrome. This is actually invalid when L−1L−1 is a multiple of 109+7109+7. The authors seem not to have noticed this and it could pass systest in contest, but three out of five solutions in contest got hacked afterwards.

What funny is that in maroon's code, he wrote something like ∗(L−1)/(L−1)∗(L−1)/(L−1), which could have caused him some trouble if the hack existed in contest.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    tosivanmak
    3 months ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    tosivanmak
    3 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    I am wondering how the solution works without realizing it is a palindrome, can you give me a brief explanation? Thanks.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      qiuzx
      3 months ago, show #
      »
      »
      »
      qiuzx
      3 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      +10

      The contribution for a single position ii (counting from backwards) is 2L−1−i×1+2L−1(L−2)L(L−1)2L−1−i×1+2L−1(L−2)L(L−1). This can be achieved by finding the pattern with brute force, or by some combinatorics calculation.

      In the end we need to maintain the length, sum of aiai, sum of i×aii×ai. The difficult part is to find out which element is deleted for every operation 11. Break the sequence into two parts from the middle. For each part, we need to insert xx and delete the first (or last) element.

      For every insertion operation, every xx inserted will be in positions s,s+d,s+2d,s+3d,⋯s,s+d,s+2d,s+3d,⋯, and a single operation either adds all ss by some value, or multiplies all dd by some value. Use a segment tree to maintain all s,ds,d, and the element deleted every time is the one with minimum ss.

      → Reply
»

        Hi Kevin! Could you share your first thoughts on Problem E? Your solution is really elegant and I'm curious about your thought process. When you first read Problem E, what was your initial intuition and what was the first thing that came to your mind when you saw the XOR operation with the constraint that all frogs must stay within the range? I'd love to know how you developed this approach of checking each bit position individually — did you start by working through small examples by hand and notice the pattern, or did you think about how XOR affects numbers at each bit level like flipping within blocks, or maybe you had some key insight that led you directly to this solution? The idea of checking whether the range is symmetric around the midpoint or covers complete blocks for each bit position is really clever, so I'm wondering how you came up with the specific conditions like r — mid == mid — l — 1 and r % i == i-1 && l % i == 0. I'm trying to improve my problem-solving skills and understanding how others go from reading a problem to finding such a clean solution would be super helpful, so thanks for sharing!

There is a slight typo in F1. The transition equation for dp[i][j][0][1]dp[i][j][0][1] and dp[i][j][1][1]dp[i][j][1][1] are not what is in the implemented solution. These are the fixed equations:

Can someone explain the bitwise approach for E?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Hayashi
    3 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    Hayashi
    3 months ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 4 →  
    0

    Define f(a,b,x,c,d,y,k)f(a,b,x,c,d,y,k) as when considering the low kk bits of a,b,x,c,d,ya,b,x,c,d,y, the number of t∈[0,2k)t∈[0,2k) such that ∀i∈[a,b]∀i∈[a,b], i⊕t≥xi⊕t≥x, and ∀j∈[c,d]∀j∈[c,d], j⊕t≤yj⊕t≤y.

    We can see the answer is exactly f(l,r,l,l,r,r,60)f(l,r,l,l,r,r,60).

    Next, consider how to calculate f(a,b,x,c,d,y,k)f(a,b,x,c,d,y,k), enumerating the kk-th bit of tt from 0 to 1, and we can express f(a,b,x,c,d,y,k)f(a,b,x,c,d,y,k) as the sum of several (possibly zero) f(.,.,.,.,.,.,k−1)f(.,.,.,.,.,.,k−1).

    Doing this with memorized search gives a correct time complexity, as when you write out all the possible value of the parameters, you will find a,x,ca,x,c will either be 00 or ll, and b,d,yb,d,y will either be 260−1260−1 or rr.

    Time complexity: O(26logr)O(26log⁡r).

    Sample code: 354930229

    → Reply

Negationist
3 months ago, show (+3) #
»
Negationist
3 months ago, hide # |
 
0

Can anyone explain more why the basis vectors can all be of the form 2^n-1? I don’t really get the motivation here.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Negationist
    3 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    Negationist
    3 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    Proof:

    Central Idea: We want so show that if a number with msb = m works then the number with all bits on that are <=m works.

    Lets define a block as a group of consecutive numbers such that any number in the group xorred with the xorring number is also in the group. It is clear that under any working xor, the range l to r will be partitioned into blocks.

    Consider any number with an msb of m that works. Consider any block in l to r that is minimal (i.e that it is not itself containing more than one block). Note that any minimal block must be self contained in some interval that is 0 to 2^(m+1)-1 under mod 2^(m+1). We want to show that the number 2^(m+1)-1 also works for this block. Since 2^(m+1)-1 just reflects numbers about the midpoint, we just need to show that the block is centered at the midpoint.

    Now, we are going to define the coordinate of a number in the modded interval by 0 to 2^(m+1)-1 in this way. [0,2^m-1] -> [-2^m, -1] and [2^m, 2^(m+1)-1] -> [1, 2^m].

    Now let us take the time to note this Lemma of Equivalent Action. Define x' as the number with the opposite coordinate as x as defined above. Suppose that x -> x + inc when xorred with a number, then clearly x' -> x'-inc.

    Now we show that any block is centered around the midpoint. Suppose that the block is not centered. Let the left of the block be l and let the right of the block be r (this is in the new coordinate system). Suppose wlog that |r| > |l| (they can't be equal since the block is not centered). Suppose that r goes to y under the xor. Note that y to r must cross the midpoint. Then note that y' is in (since it has a positive coordinate less than r) and will go to -r (by the Lemma of Equivalent Action), so -r must be in. But that contradicts that |r| > |l|, so the block is centered. If you can't quite see this, please draw a picture.

    → Reply

    »
    MohammadParsaElahimanesh

JR_OIer
3 months ago, show (+3) #

JR_OIer
3 months ago, hide # |
← Rev. 4 →  
+6

In my humble opinion, there's a small mistake in the solution of G: ∑k=1n(n−1k−1)k∑k=1n(n−1k−1)k doesn't equals to 2nn2nn but equals to 2n−1n2n−1n. Here's a brief proof:

Note that

(n−1k−1)k=(n−1)!k(n−k)!(k−1)!=n!n(n−k)!k!=(nk)n(n−1k−1)k=(n−1)!k(n−k)!(k−1)!=n!n(n−k)!k!=(nk)n

therefore, we have

∑k=1n(n−1k−1)k=∑k=1n(nk)n∑k=1n(n−1k−1)k=∑k=1n(nk)n

Binomial Theorem shows that

∑k=0n(nk)=2n∑k=0n(nk)=2n

so

∑k=1n(nk)=2n−1∑k=1n(nk)=2n−1

therefore,

∑k=1n(n−1k−1)k=2n−1n∑k=1n(n−1k−1)k=2n−1n

(apologize for my poor English)

MohammadParsaElahimanesh

→ Reply

I found the answer of question 3 is: ∑i=1nai×(i×(n−2)×2n−1+1n×(n−1)+(n−2)×2n−1+1n×(n−1)+2n−1)∑i=1nai×(i×(n−2)×2n−1+1n×(n−1)+(n−2)×2n−1+1n×(n−1)+2n−1). So I only need ∑ai∑ai and ∑i×ai∑i×ai. If something wrong, please point it out.

Then I have coded it and got WA on test 44 and 45. My code is here: 354965006. Only the main function is useful.

I found I was hacked by some thing like this: when n=k×modn=k×mod or n=k×mod+1n=k×mod+1, 1n1n or 1n−11n−1 is undefined. (Line 1231 of the code is wrong.) How to solve these two corner cases.

UPD: ops. The problem ensure n≠k×modn≠k×mod, but still 1n−11n−1 maybe undefined.

There is a minor mistake in the code for problem F1 - in the input for loop (the first for loop) which is for(int n, m, i = 0; i < T; i++), it should be for(int n, m, i = 0; i < t; i++).

Alternate stupid solution for F1:

  • Notice that if we stop at cell (i,j)(i,j), only 2⋅(i+j)2⋅(i+j) points (the ones that touch the cells on our path) determine our path, and the walls for all other points can be oriented in any of the four directions.
  • On a 5000×50005000×5000 grid, for all (i,j)(i,j), compute f(i,j)f(i,j) = the number of ways to choose a path from (1,1)(1,1) to (i,j)(i,j) and orientations for walls of the 2⋅(i+j)2⋅(i+j) points touching the cells in our chosen path. This can be easily done in O(5000⋅5000)O(5000⋅5000) (it also thankfully requires only 3⋅n23⋅n2 memory, the ML in this problem is terrible).
  • Compute g(i,j)=∑a≤i,b≤jf(a,b)⋅4−2⋅(a+b)g(i,j)=∑a≤i,b≤jf(a,b)⋅4−2⋅(a+b). This can also be easily done in O(5000⋅5000)O(5000⋅5000).
  • It's easy to see that the answer to a n×mn×m grid is g(n,m)⋅4(n+1)⋅(m+1)g(n,m)⋅4(n+1)⋅(m+1).

Code: 360970649

→ Reply
